title: Piero della Francesca, Portraits of the Duke and Duchess of Urbino (quiz)
topic_path: Piero della Francesca, Portraits of the Duke and Duchess of Urbino (quiz)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The figures in these portraits are in profile and the use of the profile was an attempt to recreate ruler portraits from [[☃ dropdown 1]].**

hints: **The figures in these portraits are in profile and the use of the profile was an attempt to recreate ruler portraits from classical antiquity.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Profile portraits are associated with piety and submission to God.**



![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c51eef1aaff60bcac8c51160ffb45289.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Profile portraits are associated with formality and power.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Renaissance women plucked their hairline because high foreheads were also thought to be [[☃ dropdown 1]].**



![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c51eef1aaff60bcac8c51160ffb45289.png)
hints: **Renaissance women plucked their hairline because high foreheads were also thought to be beautiful.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT true of Piero della Francesca's *Portraits of the Duke and Duchess of Urbino*?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c51eef1aaff60bcac8c51160ffb45289.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **These two portraits were once a diptych and connected by a hinge.**

choices.content: These kinds of portraits were only affordable to very wealthy individuals.
Federico da Montefeltro, the patron, brought Northern Renaissance painters to his court, which may have influenced the details and landscape of this painting.
The wife, Battista Sforza, had just died and this was a portrait to commemorate her.
These two portraits were once part of a larger altarpiece for the family's chapel.
